creator: list1@gjunka.com
text:
Please note the way in which the Erlang HIPE compiler uses 'opt'. It executes the command specifically to read its version number and then verifies if it's compatible with that version. If any never version of LLVM introduces any changes that are incompatible with the Erlang code my guess is that they will simply modify the code to work differently with different versions.

Please also note that Erlang doesn't depend on 'opt'. It's only that the LLVM back-end for HIPE can make use of it. If the correct version is not available HIPE can still compile using the standard non-LLVM back-end.

But it's not only about 'opt'. How would you work on a system where all tools were adding specific suffixes? For example, instead of bash you would need to execute bash43, instead of gmake - gmake41, git24 instead of git, and so on. You get the idea. I don't think Erlang is the right place to fix the incompatibility in LLVM?

How about adding shell wrappers for LLVM commands, e.g. command 'opt' would be a shell script executing the latest version of LLVM installed on the system?

Otherwise maybe devel/llvm and lang/clang are the correct solutions and they would just need to be used correctly?
